title: Lavender beeswax balm
subtitle: A small tin for the windowsill
excerpt: A two-ingredient beeswax balm with sweet almond oil, scented with English lavender. Fifty grams of finished balm, ready in under an hour, twelve months on the windowsill.
sourceNotes: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997) — beeswax-to-oil ratio reference for a soft balm. EMA HMPC monograph on Lavandula angustifolia flos (2012) — cosmetic dosing for the essential oil. Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931) — lavender entry for the traditional cosmetic use.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly to 100 g for a second tin; the 1:4 beeswax-to-oil ratio holds at any batch from 20 g to 500 g finished balm.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: No make-ahead — the recipe is one short session and the balm is ready to use as soon as it has set.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Twelve months in the sealed tin, in a cool dark cupboard. Discard if the surface develops white spots (mould) or the balm smells sharp and paint-like rather than herbal — the carrier oil has gone rancid.

recipeTools.notes: 0.1 g precision. The kitchen-scale 1 g resolution is too coarse for the essential-oil dose; round drops or volume measures are not interchangeable.
Or a heatproof bowl over a saucepan of barely simmering water.
For holding the oil + beeswax mixture at 70-75°C during the melt.
50 ml capacity. A clean jam jar with a screw lid works as a stand-in.
For folding the essential oil through the warm mixture before pouring.

glossaryTerms.definition: A preparation that contains no water phase. Anhydrous balms keep 12 months or more without a preservative because water-loving microbes have nothing to grow on.

The temperature at which a wax transitions from solid to liquid. Beeswax sits at 62-65°C; the balm melt holds at 70-75°C — comfortably above the wax melt-point and below the volatile-fragrance flash-off.
Dab a small amount on the inside of the forearm and wait 24-48 hours before wider use. Catches allergic reactions before they spread. Standard practice for any new cosmetic active.

content.text: A small tin of lavender beeswax balm. Two ingredients — sweet almond oil and beeswax — at a 4:1 ratio by weight, scented with English lavender essential oil at 1% of the finished balm. Fifteen minutes of active work, an overnight set, and the tin keeps twelve months on the windowsill.
The balm is
anhydrous

— no water phase, no preservative needed. The 4:1 oil-to-wax ratio sets to a balm that's firm in the tin but soft to a fingertip; a firmer balm uses 3:1, a softer balm uses 5:1 or 6:1. Use it on dry hands, cracked cuticles, lips, the dry patch on an elbow.
What you need

items.prepNote: cosmetic-grade sweet almond oil — refined or unrefined both work; refined is odour-neutral
cosmetic-grade pellets — easier to measure than block beeswax
about 10 drops from a standard dropper bottle — measure by weight on the precision scale for the cleanest result

content.text: The ratios state in the intro: 40 g oil + 10 g beeswax + 0.5 g essential oil = 50.5 g finished balm. Beeswax is one part to four parts oil by weight; the essential oil is 1% of the total batch. A nut allergy in the household makes sweet almond oil a no-go — substitute jojoba oil (also 40 g) or olive oil at the same weight.
Method
Weigh the oil and beeswax, melt at 70-75°C
Tare the precision scale and weigh 40 g of sweet almond oil into the top of a
double boiler
or heatproof bowl. Tare again and add 10 g of beeswax pellets to the same vessel. Set over a saucepan of barely simmering water — the water should not touch the base of the bowl.

Clip the probe thermometer to the side of the bowl with its tip in the oil but not touching the bottom. The beeswax pellets will melt into the warm oil over 8-12 minutes; the mixture should reach 70-75°C and the wax should be fully translucent. Stir once or twice with the silicone spatula to help it along. If the temperature climbs above 80°C, lift the bowl off the heat for a minute — the volatile constituents of the lavender essential oil start to flash off above 75-80°C.
Test the set on a cold saucer

Before scenting and pouring, check the set. Drip a small spoon of the warm mixture onto a cold saucer and let it sit for 30 seconds. The drop should set firm to a fingertip touch but feel soft and yielding, not waxy and hard. If it's too soft, melt in another 2 g of beeswax; if it's too firm, add 5 ml more sweet almond oil. Adjust now while the mixture is still in the bowl — you can't reset a poured tin without re-melting the whole batch.
Off the heat, fold in the essential oil

Lift the bowl off the simmering water and let the temperature drop to 65°C — the probe shows the moment. Tare the scale with the bowl on it and pipette in 0.5 g of lavender essential oil; fold through with the silicone spatula for 20 seconds. Drop-counting is approximate; weighing is exact. A standard glass dropper bottle delivers about 20 drops per millilitre, so 10 drops is roughly 0.5 ml, but viscosity varies and the scale is the truth-teller.
Pour into the tin, set 24 hours

Pour the warm mixture into the salve tin in one steady stream. Tap the tin gently on the counter to settle the surface flat. Leave the lid off for the first 10 minutes so the residual heat can off-gas — a sealed lid traps steam and condensation, which shortens the shelf life. Once the surface has dulled from glossy to matte, lid the tin and label it with the date and the herb.
The balm sets to its final firmness over 24 hours at room temperature. Tested too soon, a soft-set balm reads as under-waxed; left to cure 24 hours, the structure firms up.
Patch test on first use
On first use,
patch test

a small dab on the inside of the forearm and wait 24-48 hours before wider use. Lavender essential oil is well tolerated by most readers but skin sensitisation does happen, especially in those with a history of plant-allergy reactions.

Sweet almond oil contains tree-nut proteins; readers with a tree-nut allergy should swap it for jojoba oil (40 g) at the same weight. Lavender essential oil is on the EMA HMPC pregnancy first-trimester caution list — pregnant readers may prefer an unscented balm (omit the essential oil entirely; the 50 g of carrier + wax keeps just as well) or substitute Roman chamomile essential oil at the same 1% load.
Storage and shelf life
Twelve months in the sealed tin, in a cool dark cupboard or the bathroom cabinet away from direct sunlight. The
melt point

of beeswax is 62-65°C; a tin left in a hot car melts and re-solidifies with an oily layer on top, which is harmless but unsightly. If the surface develops white spots (mould — usually a sign water made it into the tin) or smells rancid (sharp, paint-like, distinctly not-herbal — the carrier oil has oxidised), discard the tin and start a new batch.

Date-stamping the tin makes the twelve-month window legible. A lot number (date + batch sequence, e.g. "2026-05-18-A") on every tin also helps if you're gifting — the recipient can match a batch to a date.

attrs.heading: If the balm went sideways
attrs.intro: The four ways a beeswax balm goes wrong, and what to fix.

items.symptom: Balm too soft — doesn't hold a fingertip pick-up
items.cause: Not enough beeswax for the volume of oil, or the wax didn't fully melt before pouring.
items.fix: Re-melt the balm, stir in another 2 g of beeswax pellets, test the set on a cold saucer before re-pouring.

items.symptom: Balm too hard — won't transfer to the fingertip without rubbing
items.cause: Too much beeswax for the volume of oil.
items.fix: Re-melt, add 5 ml more sweet almond oil, test the set, re-pour.

items.symptom: Surface dimples and cracks as the balm cools
items.cause: Pour temperature too low — the balm started setting before it found the tin walls.
items.fix: Re-melt, hold at 70°C, pour in one steady stream. Tap the tin to settle the surface flat before it sets.

items.symptom: Balm smells faint and waxy, not floral
items.cause: Essential oil flash-off — the lavender went in while the mixture was above 75°C and the top-notes evaporated before the balm set.
items.fix: Next batch, drop the temperature to 65°C before adding the essential oil. The 10°C buffer below the flash-point is what protects the fragrance.
